justification: Democratic legitimacy support is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning normative valuation of democracy (p33), perceived democratic performance (p34), satisfaction with democracy (p35), and current democratic assessment (p32). These four items share compatible evaluative scales (0-10 or ordered Likert) and together capture both the aspirational and experiential dimensions of legitimacy support. PCA is preferred over simple summation because the items tap related but distinct facets—importance vs. satisfaction vs. assessment—and the first component will extract the shared latent variance without assuming equal item weights.

Institutional trust and electoral confidence is best captured by combining perceptions of election quality (p41), INE's guarantee of clean processes (p42), reliability of state elections under INE (p43), vote-buying by parties (p71), and personal experience of vote-buying (p72), all of which directly index electoral integrity beliefs. The interpersonal trust items (p81_1, p82_1) are excluded because they tap a conceptually distinct generalized-trust dimension that would dilute the electoral-confidence signal. Although scales differ slightly (7-point vs. 5-point), PCA handles this by standardizing items internally, extracting the dominant shared variance across the electoral integrity cluster.

Political efficacy and engagement spans internal efficacy (belief participation is worthwhile, p25; citizens can influence government, p46), external efficacy (satisfaction with participation opportunities, p51; opinions taken into account, p52), and motivational engagement (interest in politics, p10). These five items collectively cover the canonical efficacy-engagement construct better than any single item, and PCA will isolate the shared latent dimension while down-weighting items that load on secondary factors such as political complexity (p20) or instrumental views of politics (p21), which are more attitudinal than efficacy-specific.

Authoritarian predisposition is a latent trait best measured by combining endorsement of strong leadership over laws (p61), preference for authorities acting outside rules for justice (p64), skepticism of majority-rule legitimacy (p60), prioritizing security over freedom (p17), and preference for avoiding conflict over implementing good policy (p23). These items all use 5-to-7 category ordered scales and jointly tap the core authoritarian syndrome of deference to authority, rule-bending, and conflict-aversion. PCA is appropriate because the items reflect overlapping but not identical facets of the construct, and the first component will capture the dominant authoritarian variance while accommodating minor scale differences.

All seven items share an identical 6-category scale measuring perceived influence of distinct actors, making them directly combinable. The construct of interest is the respondent's overall mental model of who holds political power, which is best summarized by the first principal component—likely contrasting elite actors (businesses, president, parties, media) against popular/civil actors (citizens, unions, church). Using all items maximizes content coverage across the actor typology, and PCA avoids the distortion that a simple mean would introduce by treating all actors as equally central to the construct.
